title: LRU Cache
description: Design a data structure that follows the Least Recently Used (LRU) cache eviction policy. Implement `get(key)`, which returns the value for `key` or `-1` if it is not present, and `put(key, value)`, which inserts or updates the key; if inserting a new key exceeds `capacity`, evict the least recently used key. Both operations must run in O(1) average time. The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["LRUCache", capacity], ["put", k, v], ["get", k], ...]`; return an array where the constructor and each `put` produce `null`, and each `get` produces its returned value.

examples.explanation: Capacity 2; key 2 evicted after put(3); key 1 evicted after put(4).

hints: Doubly-linked list + hash map combo — DLL maintain order, map gives O(1) node access.
Dummy head/tail sentinels rakh — boundary checks zero ho jaate hain.
Get bhi 'recent' bana deta hai — node ko head ke paas move kar.

title: LFU Cache
description: Design a data structure that follows the Least Frequently Used (LFU) cache eviction policy. Implement `get(key)` and `put(key, value)`, both in O(1) average time. When a new key is inserted and the cache is at `capacity`, evict the key with the smallest use frequency; if multiple keys tie on frequency, evict the least recently used among them. Both `get` and `put` on an existing key increase its frequency. The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["LFUCache", cap], ["put", k, v], ["get", k], ...]`; return an array where the constructor and each `put` produce `null`, and each `get` produces its value (`-1` if the key is absent).

examples.explanation: Key 2 evicted (freq 1) on put(3). Then key 1 evicted (freq 2 vs 3 also at 2 but 1 LRU).

hints: Freq → ordered set of keys (insertion-ordered Map works in JS).
Track `minFreq` so eviction is O(1).
On get/put existing, bump freq and move key from old freq bucket to new.

title: Min Stack
description: Design a stack that supports `push(x)`, `pop()`, `top()`, and retrieving the minimum element with `getMin()`, all in O(1) time. The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["MinStack"], ["push", x], ["pop"], ["top"], ["getMin"], ...]`; return an array where the constructor, `push`, and `pop` produce `null`, while `top` and `getMin` produce the corresponding value. `pop`, `top`, and `getMin` are only called on a non-empty stack.
constraints: pop/top/getMin called only on non-empty stack
hints: Auxiliary stack of running mins parallel to main stack.
Push only when new value <= current min; pop in lockstep when popped value === min.
Single stack with [val, min] tuples bhi kaam karta hai.

title: Implement Stack using Queues
description: Implement a last-in-first-out (LIFO) stack using only standard queue (FIFO) operations. Support `push(x)`, `pop()`, `top()`, and `empty()`. The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["MyStack"], ["push", x], ["pop"], ["top"], ["empty"]]`; return an array where the constructor and `push` produce `null`, `pop` and `top` produce the corresponding value, and `empty` produces a boolean. `pop` and `top` are only called on a non-empty stack.
constraints: pop/top called only on non-empty stack
hints: Costly push approach — har push pe q1 me daal aur q2 ke saamne rotate kar.
Result: q1's front always the most-recently pushed = stack top.
Single queue bhi kaam karta hai — rotate it after push.

title: Implement Queue using Stacks
description: Implement a first-in-first-out (FIFO) queue using only standard stack operations. Support `push(x)`, `pop()`, `peek()`, and `empty()`, with each operation running in amortized O(1) time. The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["MyQueue"], ["push", x], ["pop"], ["peek"], ["empty"]]`; return an array where the constructor and `push` produce `null`, `pop` and `peek` produce the corresponding value, and `empty` produces a boolean. `pop` and `peek` are only called on a non-empty queue.
constraints: pop/peek called only on non-empty queue
hints: Two stacks: `inStack` for pushes, `outStack` for pops.
Move from in→out only when out is empty; preserves FIFO.
Amortized O(1) — each element moves at most once between stacks.

title: Design HashSet
description: Design a hash set without using any built-in hash table library. Implement `add(key)` to insert a key, `remove(key)` to delete a key if present, and `contains(key)` to report whether the key exists. The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["MyHashSet"], ["add", k], ["remove", k], ["contains", k]]`; return an array where the constructor, `add`, and `remove` produce `null`, while `contains` produces a boolean.
hints: Bucket array of size ~1009 (prime). Each bucket is a list (chaining) of keys.
Hash = key % numBuckets.
Linear search inside the bucket — buckets stay short.

title: Time Based Key-Value Store
description: Design a time-based key-value store that records multiple values for the same key at different timestamps and retrieves the value as of a given time. Implement `set(key, value, timestamp)` to store `value` for `key` at `timestamp`, and `get(key, timestamp)` to return the value whose stored timestamp is the largest one less than or equal to the queried `timestamp`; if no such timestamp exists, return the empty string `""`. For each key, the timestamps passed to `set` are strictly increasing. The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["TimeMap"], ["set", k, v, ts], ["get", k, ts]]`; return an array where the constructor and each `set` produce `null`, and each `get` produces its returned string.
hints: Per-key array of [timestamp, value]; ts is sorted (problem guarantees).
Binary search for largest ts <= query.
Empty string when query ts < earliest stored ts.

title: Design Twitter
description: Design a simplified social media feed. Implement `postTweet(userId, tweetId)` to publish a tweet, `follow(followerId, followeeId)` and `unfollow(followerId, followeeId)` to manage follow relationships, and `getNewsFeed(userId)` to return the IDs of the 10 most recent tweets in the user's feed, ordered newest first. A user's feed includes their own tweets and the tweets of everyone they follow. The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["Twitter"], ["postTweet", u, tw], ["follow", f, t], ["unfollow", f, t], ["getNewsFeed", u]]`; return an array where the constructor, `postTweet`, `follow`, and `unfollow` produce `null`, while `getNewsFeed` produces the array of tweet IDs.
hints: Per user: list of (timestamp, tweetId).
Per user: set of followee IDs (don't forget self).
Feed: collect from self + followees, sort by ts desc, take 10.

title: Snapshot Array
description: Design an array of the given `length` whose elements all start at 0 and that supports taking immutable snapshots. Implement `set(idx, val)` to assign `val` at index `idx`, `snap()` to take a snapshot and return the new `snap_id` (snapshot ids start at 0 and increase by one each call), and `get(idx, snap_id)` to return the value at index `idx` at the time snapshot `snap_id` was taken. The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["SnapshotArray", length], ["set", i, v], ["snap"], ["get", i, snap_id]]`; return an array where the constructor and each `set` produce `null`, `snap` produces the new snapshot id, and `get` produces its value.
hints: Naive: full copy on snap — O(n) time, O(n*snaps) space. Bahut zyada.
Per-index list of [snap_id, val] writes; binary search on get.
Track current `snap_id` counter — only writes since last snap matter.

title: Design Hit Counter
description: Design a hit counter that counts the number of hits received in the past 5 minutes (300 seconds). Implement `hit(timestamp)` to record a hit at the given time (in seconds), and `getHits(timestamp)` to return the number of hits in the window `(timestamp - 300, timestamp]`. Timestamps are passed in non-decreasing order. The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["HitCounter"], ["hit", ts], ["getHits", ts]]`; return an array where the constructor and each `hit` produce `null`, and each `getHits` produces the count.
hints: Queue of timestamps. On getHits/hit, drop entries with ts <= now-300.
If timestamps cluster heavily, use bucket per second (size 300).
For follow-up: 'what if many hits per second?' — bucketed approach scales.

title: Token Bucket Rate Limiter
description: Design a token-bucket rate limiter. The bucket holds at most `cap` tokens and starts full; it refills lazily at `refillRate` tokens per second, capped at `cap`. Implement `allow(timestamp)`: when the bucket has at least one token, consume one and return `true`, otherwise return `false`. On each call, refill based on whole-second elapsed time as `tokens = min(cap, tokens + (now - lastRefill) * refillRate)`, keeping the token count as an integer. Timestamps are given in whole seconds in non-decreasing order. The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["RateLimiter", cap, refillRate], ["allow", ts]]`; return an array where the constructor produces `null` and each `allow` produces a boolean.

examples.explanation: Cap 3, refill 1/s. First 3 succeed at t=1. 4th rejected. At t=2 one token refilled, one allow ok, second rejected.

hints: Lazy refill — only update tokens when allow() is called.
tokens = min(cap, tokens + (now - lastRefillTs) * refillRate). Then update lastRefillTs = now.
Bucket starts at cap (full).

title: Design Search Autocomplete System
description: Design a search autocomplete system. The constructor receives an array of historical `sentences` and their corresponding `counts`. As a user types one character at a time, implement `input(c)`: if `c` is `'#'`, the current buffer is committed as a completed sentence (its frequency is incremented, the buffer is reset) and `[]` is returned; otherwise `c` is appended to the buffer and you return the top 3 historical sentences that start with the current buffer as a prefix, ranked by frequency descending and ties broken by ASCII order ascending. The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["AutocompleteSystem", sentences, counts], ["input", c]]`; return an array where the constructor produces `null` and each `input` produces the suggestions array.

examples.output: [null,["i love you","island","i love leetcode"],["i love you","i love leetcode"],[],[]]

constraints: Sentences contain a-z and space only
input chars are a-z, space, or #
hints: Map<sentence, freq>. Filter by prefix on each input — small data so O(N) scan ok.
Sort filtered by (-freq, sentence asc), take 3.
On '#', merge current buffer into freq map.

tests.label: commits new sentence shows in next prefix

title: Insert Delete GetRandom O(1)
description: Design a set that supports insertion, deletion, and uniform random access in O(1) average time. Implement `insert(val)`, returning `true` if the value was newly added; `remove(val)`, returning `true` if the value was present; and `getRandom()`, returning an element currently in the set. For deterministic grading, `getRandom()` must return the element at the last index of the backing array (the most recently added element still present). The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["RandomizedSet"], ["insert", x], ["remove", x], ["getRandom"]]`; return an array where the constructor produces `null`, `insert` and `remove` produce a boolean, and `getRandom` produces the selected value. `getRandom` is only called when the set is non-empty.
constraints: getRandom only called when set non-empty
hints: Array of values + Map<val, idx> for O(1) lookup.
Remove: swap-with-last, pop, update map.
getRandom — return values[values.length - 1] (deterministic per problem note).

title: Design Underground System
description: Design a system that tracks travel times between station pairs. Implement `checkIn(id, station, t)`, recording that customer `id` enters `station` at time `t`; `checkOut(id, station, t)`, recording that the same customer exits `station` at time `t` and updating the statistics for the trip from their check-in station to this one; and `getAverageTime(start, end)`, returning the average travel time over all completed trips from `start` to `end`. A customer is always checked out after checking in, and for any given `id`, times increase between check-in and check-out. The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["UndergroundSystem"], ["checkIn", id, st, t], ["checkOut", id, st, t], ["getAverageTime", a, b]]`; return an array where the constructor, `checkIn`, and `checkOut` produce `null`, and `getAverageTime` produces the average time. `getAverageTime` is always called for a pair with at least one completed trip.
constraints: 1 <= t <= 10^9, t increases per id between in/out
getAverageTime always called for a pair with at least one completed trip
hints: Per-passenger pending check-in: Map<id, [station, time]>.
Per-pair stats: Map<"a→b", [totalTime, count]>.
Average = total / count; problem tests expect integer answers so plain integer division works here.

title: Logger Rate Limiter
description: Design a logger system that rate-limits repeated messages. Implement `shouldPrintMessage(timestamp, message)`, which returns `true` if the message may be printed at the given `timestamp` and `false` otherwise; a message may be printed only if the same message has not been printed in the last 10 seconds, so after being printed at time `t` it is next allowed at `t + 10`. Also implement `purge(timestamp)`, which evicts every stored message whose last-printed time is `<= timestamp - 10` and returns the number of entries evicted. Timestamps are passed in non-decreasing order. The grader calls `runOps` with a flat operations array `ops` such as `[["Logger"], ["shouldPrintMessage", t, msg], ["purge", t]]`; return an array where the constructor produces `null`, `shouldPrintMessage` produces a boolean, and `purge` produces the evicted count.
hints: Map<msg, lastPrintedTs>. Allowed if !has(msg) or t - lastTs >= 10.
Purge: iterate map, delete entries where ts <= now - 10.
Update map only on a successful print.
